concise:
Ayah 1–26 · linked video: 01. Al-Ghashiyah (Ayah 1-26)

Surah Al-Ghashiyah: The Overwhelming Event

Ayah 1

هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ ٱلْغَـٰشِيَةِ

Has the news of the Overwhelming Event come to you?

Allah ﷻ begins this surah by addressing His Prophet ﷺ directly: “Has the news of Al-Ghashiyah come to you?” This opening question is significant in Arabic literary style. When such a question is posed, it doesn’t express uncertainty—rather, it asks the listener to reconsider something they already know, as if encountering it for the first time. Though the Prophet ﷺ had already received extensive descriptions of Judgment Day, Allah ﷻ asks him to contemplate this reality anew, with fresh awareness.

The word الغاشية carries profound meaning. Derived from the root غشي, it refers to something that covers, wraps up, or overtakes completely. غشاء in Arabic means a blanket or cover that envelops entirely. When applied to Judgment Day, this term conveys an inescapable reality—there will be no refuge, no escape from what is coming. It will hover over people’s minds, consuming their entire consciousness. They won’t be able to think about anything else or find any distraction from what they’re experiencing.

Some scholars note that this word also suggests a state where the mind becomes overwhelmed to the point of feeling lost, similar to how the Quran describes people on that Day: “And you will see people as if they are intoxicated, but they are not intoxicated; rather, the punishment of Allah is severe” (22:2). The تاء المربوطة at the end—a grammatical feature common to words describing catastrophic events like الطامة and القارعة—indicates something that happens once and for all, a final and decisive event with no second wave or repetition.

An important question arises: Why does Allah ﷻ address His Prophet ﷺ directly when everyone else needs to hear this message? This is a subtle psychological device employed throughout the Quran. Allah ﷻ is fully aware that the audience can hear Him, but by addressing the Messenger ﷺ instead of them directly, He expresses a certain frustration with those who refuse to listen. It’s as if Allah ﷻ is saying to His Prophet ﷺ: “Haven’t I taught you this enough? Haven’t they heard this reminder repeatedly? Yet still they don’t respond.” This indirect address actually intensifies the message’s impact on those who are truly listening.

Ayah 2

وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَـٰشِعَةٌ

Some faces that Day will be humbled.

Allah ﷻ now takes us into the scene of that Day, and notice how He describes people—not by their bodies or their actions, but by their faces. وُجُوهٌ—some faces—will be خاشعة, humbled and overwhelmed. The face is the mirror of the soul; everything happening internally manifests on one’s face. Fear, anger, sadness, regret, happiness, exhaustion—all these emotions reveal themselves immediately in facial expressions. You can tell when someone is sick just by looking at their face, by the bags under their eyes or the color of their skin.

The word خاشعة expresses humiliation, powerlessness, and being completely overwhelmed. In this life, خشوع is actually a beautiful quality—it’s the humility and awe we’re supposed to feel in prayer and in our relationship with Allah ﷻ. But this same quality becomes a mark of disgrace on Judgment Day for those who rejected faith. It’s as if Allah ﷻ has flipped the equation: those who voluntarily humbled themselves before Allah ﷻ in this world will not experience this involuntary humiliation in the next. But those who refused to humble themselves here, who felt no خشوع before their Creator, will experience it forcibly in the Hereafter.

The Quran describes this elsewhere: “You will see them being presented before it, humbled from humiliation, looking from a concealed glance” (42:45). Their eyes will be exhausted, unable to make eye contact, overwhelmed by what they’re witnessing. Even before any judgment has been passed, before any books have been opened or records examined, their faces will betray their guilt. Like a criminal in a room who immediately changes expression when police enter—everyone else wonders what’s happening, but the guilty person knows exactly why they’re there.

Ayah 3

عَامِلَةٌ نَّاصِبَةٌ

Working hard, exhausted.

These faces will be عاملة ناصبة—working and exhausted. Both words describe extreme exhaustion from labor. The word ناصبة comes from نصب, which means to lean over from weariness, or to be pegged down like something driven into the ground. This is the same word used when the Prophet ﷺ is commanded to exhaust himself in worship: فَإِذَا فَرَغْتَ فَانصَبْ (94:7).

But here’s the striking paradox: these people were sleeping in their graves. They’ve had a long rest. When they wake up on Judgment Day, shouldn’t they look refreshed? Why do they appear overworked and exhausted? The answer is profound: their time in the grave was not peaceful rest. For the disbeliever, even the grave is a place of exhaustion and torment. They wake up already drained from what they experienced in the barzakh, the period between death and resurrection.

This is one of the Quran’s subtle references to the life of the grave. While some claim the Quran doesn’t mention what happens in the grave, verses like this allude to that reality. The fact that these faces appear exhausted on a day when they should be rested indicates they’ve been through something during their time beneath the earth.

There’s also a deeper contrast being established here. These are the people who lived for ease and pleasure in the worldly life, avoiding any exhaustion for Allah’s sake. Now they face eternal exhaustion. Meanwhile, the believers who exhausted themselves in worship and obedience in this temporary life will find eternal rest. The equation is simple: either you volunteer yourself into exhaustion for Allah ﷻ in this brief life and rest forever, or you rest from His obedience now and face exhaustion forever.

Ayah 4

تَصْلَىٰ نَارًا حَامِيَةً

Burning in a scorching Fire.

These faces will be تصلى نارا حامية—cast into a scorching fire. The verb تصلى suggests being thrown or cast, and the description of the fire as حامية requires careful attention. At first glance, it seems obvious—of course fire is hot. Why emphasize that it’s “scorching”? But this emphasis serves a crucial purpose in Arabic: it indicates the extreme, the maximum degree. This isn’t just fire; this is fire that has reached the peak of its heat, fire that couldn’t possibly burn any hotter.

Throughout this surah, you’ll notice Allah ﷻ uses adjectives that might seem to state the obvious—flowing water, placed cups, raised beds. But each of these emphasizes the extreme: the water couldn’t flow any more beautifully, the arrangement couldn’t be any more perfect, the fire couldn’t burn any more intensely. The حامية description tells us this fire has reached maximum temperature, held back only by Allah’s permission from going even further.

Ayah 5

تُسْقَىٰ مِنْ عَيْنٍ ءَانِيَةٍ

They will be given drink from a boiling spring.

Now comes a cruel irony. Those burning faces desperately need relief, and the natural relief from fire is water. So Allah ﷻ says they will be given to drink from عين—a spring. For the Arabs living in the desert, the word عين is beautiful. It means “eye,” but it also refers to anything that captures the eye with its beauty. A wellspring, a fountain—these were the most precious sights in the barren desert. The word عين has over seventy different meanings in Arabic, all connected to beauty and value.

So when you hear تُسقَىٰ مِنْ عَيْنٍ, there’s a momentary sense of relief—water! But then comes the next word that snatches away any comfort: آنية. This word comes from أنى, meaning “now”—the moment when water reaches its highest boiling point. When you heat water and watch the bubbles become violent, when you instinctively say “now, turn it off, turn it off!”—that critical moment is called آنية. This is water at maximum boiling temperature, bubbling violently.

Picture the scene: these faces are burning in maximum heat, and when they seek relief, their entire faces are forced into springs of violently boiling water. The torment is layered, unrelenting. Some scholars note an alternative reading: تَسْقَى instead of تُسْقَى, suggesting these people will voluntarily throw their faces into the boiling water—such is the intensity of the fire that even boiling water seems like relief.

Ayah 6

لَّيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعَامٌ إِلَّا مِن ضَرِيعٍ

They will have no food except from ضريع.

Now the torment extends internally. ليس لهم طعام—they won’t have any طعام, which isn’t just “food” but nourishing food, sustenance that actually feeds you. They’ll have nothing except ضريع.

What is dari’? Scholars offer several explanations. Some say it’s a type of raw seaweed. But the stronger interpretation, found in classical Arabic literature, identifies it as a thorny, onion-like plant. When fresh, camels might eat it, but when it dries up (and the word ضريع comes from the root indicating something dried), it becomes poisonous, bitter, and deadly. It causes extreme stomach pain and can kill. This dried, toxic plant is scattered throughout Hell, and it’s the only “food” available.

Imagine the scene: these people are experiencing unbearable hunger on top of the burning. They wander the terrain of Hell desperately searching for something—anything—to eat. They find nothing except this dried, poisonous plant, and in their desperation, they keep stuffing it into their mouths.

The use of the word طعام is almost sarcastic here, just as عين was sarcastic in the previous verse. How can this poisonous substance be called “food”? But that’s all there is. Some scholars suggest dari’ might be a proper name for the fruit of the tree of زقوم mentioned elsewhere in the Quran, since that’s the only tree that produces anything edible (if you can call it that) in Jahannam.

Ayah 7

لَّا يُسْمِنُ وَلَا يُغْنِى مِن جُوعٍ

Which neither nourishes nor satisfies hunger.

Allah ﷻ continues describing this “food”: لا يسمن—it doesn’t fatten them, it doesn’t fill their stomachs. ولا يغني من جوع—and it doesn’t free them from hunger, not even slightly. The word يغني means to make someone independent of something, to relieve them of a need. This food doesn’t alleviate their starvation even a little.

Some might have heard this description and thought, “Well, we’ve seen dried plants before. How bad could it be?” But Allah ﷻ adds these details to show: no matter how disgusting it tastes, no matter how poisonous, no matter how painful it is to consume, they’ll keep desperately trying to fill their stomachs with it. And it won’t help. Their appetite won’t decrease. The hunger won’t subside. The torture is both external (the fire on their skin) and internal (the starvation and poisoning inside their bodies), and neither finds any relief.

Ayah 8

وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاعِمَةٌ

Other faces that Day will be radiant with bliss.

After this intense description of torment, a natural question arises in any listener’s mind: “Is there any good news? Please, tell us something positive.” And Allah ﷻ immediately switches the scene. This is the relief mentioned at the beginning—a brief respite in the surah’s dark intensity.

وجوه يومئذ ناعمة—some faces that Day will be na’imah. The word ناعمة has beautiful layers of meaning. First, it describes faces that are soft, relaxed, comforted. When you look at something that brings you peace, your face reflects that comfort. The tension disappears. Think of the expression on someone’s face during a soothing massage—that’s the idea of na’imah.

But ناعمة also means experiencing luxury and beauty. It’s the face someone makes when they witness something breathtakingly beautiful. You’ve seen this in real life—someone walks into a magnificent space and their face lights up with wonder. That expression, that involuntary response to overwhelming beauty, is captured in na’imah.

Interestingly, the same root gives us the word for the expression you make when receiving a cherished gift—not a practical gift you didn’t want, but something you truly desired. That joy, that grateful surprise, is all contained in this word na’imah.

Ayah 9

لِّسَعْيِهَا رَاضِيَةٌ

Pleased with their effort.

Now Allah ﷻ explains why these faces are so radiant: لسعيها راضية—they are pleased because of their efforts. The word سعي means urgent, dedicated effort. These faces that now appear so relaxed and joyful—they were the stressed faces in the worldly life. They were the ones who exhausted themselves staying within Allah’s boundaries, avoiding His prohibitions, striving in worship.

Notice the beautiful contrast: the word ناصبة (exhausted) was used for the people of the Fire. The concept of sa’y (exhaustive effort) is used here for the people of Paradise. Both groups experienced exhaustion, but in opposite directions. One group exhausted themselves chasing temporary pleasures and running from Allah’s commands. The other exhausted themselves in obedience and worship. The first group’s exhaustion led to eternal torment; the second group’s exhaustion led to eternal bliss.

The preposition ل here is crucial. The translation isn’t “pleased with their efforts” as if they’re proud of themselves. Rather, it’s “pleased because of their efforts”—pleased that Allah ﷻ gave them the opportunity to strive, grateful that they were able to make those efforts. Right now, when we’re going through struggles and tests, we sometimes feel like complaining. But on that Day, we’ll be thanking Allah ﷻ that we went through those struggles, because those very struggles are why we’re experiencing this bliss.

The word راضية suggests complete contentment, total satisfaction. Looking back at their lives, seeing what their efforts earned them, they’ll wish every single moment had been spent in Allah’s service. No time will seem wasted. And this contentment isn’t just with the reward—it’s contentment with Allah ﷻ Himself for having blessed them with the ability to strive in His path.

Ayah 10

فِى جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ

In an elevated Garden.

These blessed faces find themselves في جنة عالية—in an elevated Garden. The word عالية emphasizes height and elevation. In this world, Allah ﷻ says, “We raise in degrees whom We will” (12:76). Now that raising of ranks manifests literally—they’re elevated into high Gardens.

But what’s the significance of a high garden versus a low one? The elevated position provides the ultimate view. Imagine being placed in a garden on a height where everywhere you look, you see Paradise spreading before you—the entire heavenly landscape visible from your vantage point. It’s like having the best seat in the house, the royal box overlooking everything.

This elevation also evokes kingship and honor. In ancient times, elevated seating was reserved for royalty. The king sat on a raised throne to view his entire court and to be visible to all. This imagery continues in how weddings are conducted even today—the bride and groom placed on a stage in elevated seats, treated like royalty for that occasion. These believers are being given that royal treatment, that honored position for eternity.

Ayah 11

لَّا تَسْمَعُ فِيهَا لَـٰغِيَةً

In it you will hear no worthless talk.

لا تسمع فيها لاغية—you won’t hear any laghw, any worthless or vain speech. The word لغو refers to speech that has no benefit whatsoever, talk that serves no purpose.

Now, there are two ways to understand this verse. One interpretation addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly: Right now, you have to endure so much nonsense, so many terrible things said to you when you preach this message. But a time will come when you’ll never have to hear any of that again. This provides comfort to the Messenger ﷺ and, by extension, to all believers who face mockery and harsh words for their faith.

The other interpretation refers back to those radiant faces—they won’t hear any worthless talk. Some people worry: “Does this mean I can’t have fun conversations in Paradise? I won’t be able to joke around?” But understand what laghw really means. It’s not just casual conversation—it’s speech that has absolutely no possible benefit, speech that only leads to harm or waste.

Here’s the beautiful reality: In this life, things that seem entertaining and pleasurable often turn out to be harmful once we experience them. They promise benefit but deliver harm—it’s a bait and switch. But in Paradise, everything—even our pleasures, even our entertainment—carries benefit. Our enjoyments don’t come at anyone else’s expense. There’s no greed, no jealousy, no coveting what others have, because everything that belongs to anyone else also belongs to you, in infinite measure.

The Quran promises: “We will remove whatever ill-feeling they had in their hearts” (7:43). All negative emotions are gone. That’s why there’s no empty speech—because there’s no negativity to express, no harm to cause, no time to waste. Every conversation, every interaction, every moment is elevated and meaningful.

Ayah 12

فِيهَا عَيْنٌ جَارِيَةٌ

In it is a flowing spring.

فيها عين جارية—there’s a wellspring in it, and it’s flowing. Earlier we saw عين آنية, a boiling spring of torment. Now we see عين جارية, a beautifully flowing spring.

Why mention that it flows? Isn’t that obvious—springs flow? But the description جارية emphasizes freshness. The Arabs would desperately search for water in the desert, and if they found a well with stagnant water, it could mean death—the water had gone bad. Fresh, flowing water meant life. The Quran describes Paradise as having “rivers of water never changing in taste” (47:15)—the constant flow ensures perpetual freshness.

Imagine sitting by this wellspring, the water flowing beautifully, splashing your face with it, refreshing yourself. The entire scene is one of luxury and comfort, a far cry from the boiling springs of Jahannam.

Ayah 13

فِيهَا سُرُرٌ مَّرْفُوعَةٌ

In it are couches raised high.

فيها سرر مرفوعة—there are beds and couches that are raised, elevated. The word سرير refers to a couch or bed, and مرفوعة means raised or elevated.

Now, beds are generally raised anyway—that’s their nature. So why emphasize that they’re raised? The emphasis indicates they couldn’t be raised any better, any higher, any more perfectly. This is the ultimate in comfort and luxury. Also, the raised seating allows you to take in the full view of your surroundings in Paradise, reinforcing the imagery of the elevated garden from earlier.

The Arabs, particularly the Bedouins, didn’t have elaborate furniture. They were nomadic, always on the move, so they used simple bedding and cushions that could be easily packed and carried by camel. They couldn’t invest in proper furniture, in raised beds and couches. So this description promises them what they never had—permanent residence, stability, and the finest furniture imaginable. No more packing up and moving. This is home, forever, and it’s furnished with the best.

Ayah 14

وَأَكْوَابٌ مَّوْضُوعَةٌ

And cups already placed.

وأكواب موضوعة—and cups that are placed, set out and ready. The word أكواب refers to expensive goblets, ornate cups made of gold or silver, decorated beautifully. These aren’t ordinary drinking vessels—these are cups fit for royalty.

But again, why mention that they’re “placed”? Cups are always placed somewhere, aren’t they? The description موضوعة paints a specific scene: you’re sitting on your raised couch, relaxed, taking in the view, and you think, “I’d like something to drink.” You don’t have to get up. The drink is already there, already placed within reach. Later verses in the Quran fill in more details—servants come and pour for you, attending to your every need before you even have to ask.

Think about the difference between self-service and luxury service. When you go to a fast-food place, you get your own cup, fill your own drink, throw away your own trash. But at an expensive banquet, everything is already set for you—the glasses placed upside down on crisp tablecloths, the server dressed formally coming to pour for you, refilling your glass before it’s even empty. That level of service where you don’t lift a finger—that’s what’s being described here.

Ayah 15

وَنَمَارِقُ مَصْفُوفَةٌ

And cushions lined up in rows.

ونمارق مصفوفة—and cushions arranged in rows. نمارق are cushions or pillows, and مصفوفة means arranged in rows, lined up neatly.

Why rows and rows of cushions? Because where there are many cushions arranged, there are many guests. This is describing a gathering, a party, a social scene. One of the great joys of Paradise is companionship—being with people you genuinely enjoy, having elevated conversations, sharing experiences.

Picture the scene building: you’re sitting on raised couches by a flowing fountain, cups of drink already placed before you, cushions arranged all around, other believers gathered together. Everyone’s face is radiant. You’re all exchanging stories: “SubhanAllah, you made it! I remember when we struggled together back in the world. Remember that time we…” These beautiful conversations, this elevated company, this is part of the bliss of Paradise.

Ayah 16

وَزَرَابِىُّ مَبْثُوثَةٌ

And fine carpets spread out.

وزرابي مبثوثة—and carpets that are spread out, displayed. Here’s something fascinating: زرابي isn’t actually an Arabic word. It’s a loan word referring to luxury rugs imported from Azerbaijan, which was famous for making exquisite carpets and fine crafts.

Think about this: when you want to describe something really luxurious today, you might reference Italian cars, Swiss watches, or Persian rugs—items known for their quality and associated with specific places. The Arabs did the same thing. When they wanted to describe the finest rugs, they referenced imports from Azerbaijan. These were exotic, expensive, the kind of thing only the wealthy could afford.

But here’s the key: Allah ﷻ isn’t saying you’ll literally get rugs from Azerbaijan in Paradise. Rather, He’s using the language His audience understands to help them imagine something beyond imagination. It’s as if He’s saying, “Remember the most exotic, luxurious carpet you’ve ever seen or heard about? Now imagine something even more magnificent, something that makes those earthly luxuries look pale in comparison.”

The Quran does this elsewhere too. In Surah Ar-Rahman, Allah ﷻ describes luxury items as عَبْقَرِيّ, which comes from a legendary land where jinn were said to create magical objects. In other words, items so exotic and beautiful they seem from another world. That’s what awaits in Paradise—furnishings, luxuries, experiences that are genuinely out of this world.

This is important to understand: Allah ﷻ wants us to imagine what Paradise will be like, but He knows our imagination is limited by what we’ve experienced. So He uses our best earthly experiences as a springboard, knowing that the reality of Paradise exceeds anything we could possibly conceive. As the Prophet ﷺ said, “No eye has seen, no ear has heard, and no human heart has imagined what Allah has prepared for the believers.”

Ayah 17

أَفَلَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَى ٱلْإِبِلِ كَيْفَ خُلِقَتْ

Do they not look at the camels—how they were created?

Now comes what seems like an abrupt shift, but it’s actually the heart of the entire surah. After describing the horrors of Jahannam and the bliss of Paradise, Allah ﷻ poses a question: أفلا ينظرون إلى الإبل كيف خلقت—Have they not looked at the camels, how they were created?

The word ينظرون means more than a casual glance. It means to stare, to observe carefully, to really examine. And when do people have time to stare at a camel for extended periods? When they’re traveling. The Arab traveler would spend days, weeks, even months on journey, and during all that time, what’s in front of them? The camel they’re riding.

During those long journeys, if they really observed, they’d notice incredible things about this creature. How gentle it is despite its size and strength. How high you can sit on it, giving you a view of the terrain. How it survives on almost nothing—minimal food and water—in an environment where most creatures would perish. How it provides milk for drink, skin for shelter, meat for food. This is a creature perfectly designed as a “ship of the desert,” engineered specifically for navigating that harsh landscape. A horse couldn’t survive these conditions. No other animal could fulfill this role. But the camel does, perfectly.

Look at the engineering: its rough skin can withstand sandstorms and, when the camel dies, can be fashioned into a tent that protects humans from those same storms. Its meat can sustain a family for weeks. But perhaps most remarkable are its eyes—the camel has a natural windshield wiper system. When sandstorms come, humans have to cover their faces and eyes, but the camel doesn’t blink. Allah ﷻ designed its eyelids to block sand particles while allowing it to keep moving through the storm. Have you really noticed this incredible mechanical design? This creature exists for your benefit, for your survival.

Allah ﷻ is teaching a profound lesson here: Look at this magnificent creature I’ve placed in your service. You don’t even think twice about using it, riding it, benefiting from it. Yet you refuse to place yourselves in My service? This amazing creation submits to you completely—will you not submit to your Creator?

There’s something else embedded in this imagery. The theme is journey—and it’s during a journey that you’d stare at a camel for so long. Your entire life is a journey, and every journey has a destination. You’re traveling through this world; where will you end up?

Ayah 18

وَإِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ كَيْفَ رُفِعَتْ

And at the sky—how it is raised?

وإلى السماء كيف رفعت—And at the sky, how it has been raised? Now the traveler’s eye moves from the camel upward to the vast sky above. كيف رفعت—how has it been elevated, lifted up?

When you’re traveling through the desert, especially at night, the sky becomes an overwhelming presence. It stretches endlessly in every direction, this magnificent ceiling suspended over you. The stars shine with clarity you never see in the city. You feel simultaneously small beneath its vastness and connected to something infinite.

What put this beautiful roof over you? Who suspended it without pillars? Who designed it to protect you, to provide light during the day and stars for navigation at night? The sky reminds the traveler: you’re always under watch. There’s no hiding from what’s above you. And just as this sky covers you now, one day you’ll be under the earth, and then one day you’ll stand before the One who created both.

Ayah 19

وَإِلَى ٱلْجِبَالِ كَيْفَ نُصِبَتْ

And at the mountains—how they are erected?

وإلى الجبال كيف نصبت—And at the mountains, how they’ve been erected, pegged into the ground? As your eye comes down from the sky, you see in the distance ranges of mountains. The word نصبت is the same root as ناصبة from earlier—exhausted, pegged down.

Notice the connection: those arrogant faces on Judgment Day will be ناصبة, exhausted and humbled. But look at these mountains—Allah ﷻ has already pegged them down, grounded them firmly to the earth. If He can humble and fix in place these massive, immovable mountains, what makes you think you can escape His power? What makes you think you won’t be brought low?

The mountains also teach humility in another way. They’re ancient, permanent, towering—yet they don’t move. They stand exactly where Allah ﷻ placed them. They don’t resist or rebel. They submit. And you, human being, with your short life and fragile frame—you’re resisting your Creator?

Ayah 20

وَإِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَيْفَ سُطِحَتْ

And at the earth—how it is spread out?

وإلى الأرض كيف سطحت—And at the earth, how it has been spread out, made level and smooth? Finally, the eye comes to rest on the earth itself, the ground beneath you. كيف سطحت—how it’s been flattened, made traversable?

Nobody appreciates flat, level land more than the desert traveler. Try walking across rocky, uneven terrain for hours—it’s exhausting, dangerous, slow. But smooth, flat earth allows you to cover distance, to journey safely, to reach your destination. Allah ﷻ has prepared the earth as a smooth path for you.

This is especially significant in the Arabian Peninsula, where vast stretches of flat desert allow for travel. Without this, trade wouldn’t be possible, migration wouldn’t happen, entire civilizations couldn’t have developed. The smooth earth is a mercy, a gift that often goes unnoticed.

These four observations—the camel, the sky, the mountains, the earth—capture the reality surrounding the Arab traveler. Together, they should inspire reflection: Someone designed all of this. Someone created this system perfectly suited for your life, your survival, your journey. These signs are everywhere, obvious to anyone who really looks. The camel engineered for your benefit, the sky elevated above you, the mountains grounded firmly, the earth smoothed for your passage—all of it testifies to a Creator, a Designer, a Lord worthy of worship.

But there’s an urgent undertone here. Remember Surah Al-Qari’ah and other descriptions of Judgment Day—the sky will be torn open, the mountains will crumble and scatter, the earth will convulse, the animals will be abandoned. All of this that’s stable and normal right now won’t remain that way. You’re on a journey through a world that has an expiration date. These signs around you are reminders: You still have time. The earth is still smooth, the sky still stable, the mountains still standing, the camel still serving you. But that won’t last forever. Use this time wisely.

Ayah 21

فَذَكِّرْ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُذَكِّرٌ

So remind, for you are only a reminder.

Now comes the conclusion addressed directly to the Prophet ﷺ: فذكر إنما أنت مذكر—So remind, you are only one who reminds. The فاء here connects back to everything that came before. Since these signs are everywhere, since the evidence is clear to anyone who thinks, your job is straightforward: remind.

إنما أنت مذكر—you are nothing but a reminder. That’s your entire role. You’re not responsible for changing people. You’re not in charge of making them believe. You just remind. If they’re people who think, who observe, who contemplate—your reminder will connect with what they already see around them, and the truth will become clear. But if they refuse to think, if they ignore the signs, if they close their minds—that’s not your responsibility.

Notice that the specific content of the reminder isn’t mentioned. What should he remind them of? The Hereafter? The signs of Allah ﷻ in creation? The stories of previous nations? The answer: all of it. Whatever reminder is given, if the recipient is someone of contemplation, it will all converge and make sense. The pieces will come together. But you can’t force that to happen.

This is profoundly liberating for anyone who tries to share Islam. Your job isn’t to control outcomes. Your job isn’t to force belief. Your job is simply to remind, to convey the message. The previous surah, Al-A’la, emphasized the same thing: “So remind, if the reminder benefits” (87:9). Whether people benefit from it or not, you still give the reminder. That’s your duty, and that’s the limit of your duty.

Ayah 22

لَّسْتَ عَلَيْهِم بِمُصَيْطِرٍ

You are not over them a controller.

لست عليهم بمصيطر—You are not a controller over them. The word مصيطر means someone who has complete control, someone who guards and manages others with authority. You don’t have that power over people. You can’t force them to believe. You can’t compel them to change.

This is the attitude every Muslim should have when conveying Islam to others. We don’t control people. We don’t get frustrated when they don’t listen. We don’t take it as a personal failure. We simply present the message, and we leave the results to Allah ﷻ.

Think about it: you share an ayah with a family member, and they don’t listen. You feel disappointed, maybe even angry. But Prophet Nuh (AS) gave da’wah for 950 years, and only a handful believed. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was rejected by his own people for years. If prophets faced rejection, why would we expect different? Sometimes our frustration is more about our ego—they didn’t listen to me—than about the message itself.

The reality is, Allah ﷻ won’t change people until they change what’s within themselves: “Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves” (13:11). That’s the central message of this surah. If their inner perspective hasn’t changed, if how they look at reality hasn’t transformed, then no amount of reminders will change them externally.

Ayah 23

إِلَّا مَن تَوَلَّىٰ وَكَفَرَ

Except whoever turns away and disbelieves.

إلا من تولى وكفر—Except the one who turns away and disbelieves. This exception has been interpreted in two significant ways.

First interpretation: You have no authority over them now—but eventually, you will. Mecca will be conquered. The time is coming when you will have authority over these very people who reject you today. And if they still don’t believe even then, even after your authority is established, then Allah ﷻ will deal with them. This reading sees the exception as a promise to the Prophet ﷺ: vindication is coming.

The second interpretation: You have no control over them in this life, but there’s an exception to their freedom—those who turn away and persist in disbelief will face consequences in the Hereafter. They may think they’re escaping now, but there’s no escaping what comes next.

Both interpretations work, and the beauty of the Quran is that both meanings can be intended simultaneously.

Ayah 24

فَيُعَذِّبُهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَكْبَرَ

Then Allah will punish them with the greatest punishment.

فيعذبه الله العذاب الأكبر—Then Allah ﷻ will punish them with the greatest punishment. Why not just “punishment”? Why العذاب الأكبر—the greatest of all punishments?

This requires careful thought. Who is this referring to? Is it everyone who disbelieves? Or is it specifically those who directly encountered the Prophet ﷺ, who lived with him, heard him, knew his character—and still turned away?

The Prophet ﷺ is the greatest gift Allah ﷻ ever gave to humanity. The Quran is the ultimate revelation. The combination of the perfect message delivered through the perfect messenger—that’s the greatest opportunity for truth that has ever been presented to human beings. Those who witnessed this directly, who saw the Prophet ﷺ with their own eyes, heard his recitation with their own ears, knew his honesty, his character, his mercy—and still rejected him and became his enemies—their crime is unmatched.

People who come later, who don’t believe, are still accountable. But their crime doesn’t compare to those who knew the Prophet ﷺ personally and still rejected him. Those who had the greatest opportunity and refused it deserve العذاب الأكبر, the greatest punishment.

This makes us sensitive to how the Quran discusses punishment. Not every mention of punishment applies equally to everyone. Allah ﷻ makes distinctions, and we need to be careful about passing blanket judgments. Context matters. Circumstances matter. The degree of knowledge and opportunity matters.

The word عذاب interestingly comes from the root meaning extreme thirst, which connects back to the imagery earlier in the surah—the burning, the desperate thirst, the boiling water that provides no relief. The punishment manifests as unquenchable thirst while being surrounded by water that burns.

Ayah 25

إِنَّ إِلَيْنَآ إِيَابَهُمْ

Indeed, to Us is their return.

إن إلينا إيابهم—Indeed, to Us is their return. The word إياب means to return, but specifically to return with humility, to come back in a state of submission. Let them go wherever they want in this life. Let them mock, let them dismiss, let them make themselves enemies of the truth. It’s fine. Eventually, they’re coming back, and there’s nowhere else they can go. They will return, humbled.

This connects beautifully back to the opening of the surah. Remember خاشعة—faces that are humbled? And now إياب—returning with humility. They refused humility in this life; they’ll experience it by force in the next. The surah is coming full circle.

The إن at the beginning emphasizes certainty. There’s no doubt about this. إلينا—to Us specifically, not to anyone else. They might think they can escape, but their ultimate destination is fixed. They’re coming back to Allah ﷻ, and only to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 26

ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا حِسَابَهُم

Then indeed, upon Us is their reckoning.

ثم إن علينا حسابهم—Then indeed, upon Us is their account, their reckoning. Not only will they return to Us with humility, but We alone will audit them. We alone will hold them accountable for everything they did.

The word حساب means a detailed accounting, an audit. Every action, every word, every intention—all of it will be reviewed. Nothing will be missed. And Allah ﷻ emphasizes: علينا—upon Us. We have taken this responsibility. No one else will judge them. No one else will pass verdict. Only Allah ﷻ.

This concluding statement reinforces the opening: the Ghashiyah, the overwhelming event that covers everything, that no one can escape—it’s real, it’s coming, and Allah ﷻ alone controls it.

Structural Beauty of the Surah

Looking at the surah as a whole, notice its careful construction:

Opening: The Prophet ﷺ is addressed directly about the Overwhelming Event.
Closing: The Prophet ﷺ is addressed directly about his role as a reminder.

Opening: Faces will be humbled (خاشعة).
Closing: They will return with humility (إياب).

Opening: They face inescapable punishment.
Closing: They will return to Allah ﷻ and only He will judge them—there’s no escape.

The middle section—the relief about Paradise—appears briefly, then we return to the main theme. And at the heart of the surah is the call to observation and reflection: Look at the camel, the sky, the mountains, the earth. Think. Contemplate. Each of these teaches something essential for the journey of life.

The camel teaches gratitude—look what Allah ﷻ created for your benefit. The sky teaches awareness—you’re always under watch. The mountains teach humility—if Allah ﷻ can humble these massive structures, He can humble you. The earth teaches your temporary nature—it’s smooth now for your journey, but you’ll be beneath it soon.

The profound message is this: the reminder from the Prophet ﷺ will only benefit those who become people of thought, people who observe and reflect on the reality around them. If someone looks at creation and thinks deeply, the reminders of revelation will resonate. They’ll connect. Everything will make sense. But if someone refuses to think, refuses to observe, closes their mind—then no amount of reminding will help.

May Allah ﷻ make us people of deep thought and reflection. May He make us contemplate His signs in creation and in revelation. May He soften our hearts to His reminders and save us from being among those whose faces are humbled in disgrace on the Day of Judgment. And may He grant us radiant faces, pleased with our efforts, dwelling in elevated gardens for eternity. Ameen.

Ayah 1–26 · linked video: 01. Al-Ghashiyah (Ayah 1-26)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

هَلْ (hal) [ه-ل]

This interrogative particle is used not to ask a genuine question, but rather as a rhetorical device to make the listener reconsider something they already know. In Arabic literature, when you ask “did this happen?” in this manner, you are actually saying “I know you have heard about it, now I want you to rethink it and review it in your mind as if it never happened before.” The listener is being told to remember something and revisit that memory anew.

أَتَاكَ (ataka) [أ-ت-ي]

The root أ-ت-ي means to come or to arrive. The verb form here (أَتَاكَ) addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم directly in the second person masculine singular. The use of حَدِيثُ (hadith) with this verb is significant because hadith in Arabic is used for something new that happens, a new event that takes place. Even though so much description of Judgment Day has already been given to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم, he is being asked here to consider what will happen on Judgment Day as if he is just learning about it right now.

الْغَاشِيَةِ (al-ghashiyah) [غ-ش-ي]

The root غ-ش-ي means to cover, wrap up, or overtake entirely. The word is derived from something that overtakes, covers, and wraps up completely. غِشَاوَة (ghishawah) is a blanket or cover that covers you entirely. This word is used allegorically to describe Judgment Day - it will cover everybody and there will be no escape. It will hover over minds and take over completely. Some say it refers to the point where people will feel like they are going insane. The word الغاشية is also used when the mind is lost, like people cannot believe what is happening in front of their eyes. The ة (ta marbuta) at the end is common in the Quran for catastrophic events (like الطَّامَّة, الصَّاخَّة, الْقَارِعَة). The ta marbuta does something called تَفْعِيل الْمَرَّة (taf’il al-marrah), indicating something that happens once and for all - this one covering event that will happen and that is it, the final one.

Ayah 2

وُجُوهٌ (wujuhun) [و-ج-ه]

The root و-ج-ه refers to the face. Instead of describing people, Allah describes faces in this Surah. When you see somebody’s face, you know everything that is going on with them. Fear, anger, sadness, regret, happiness, exhaustion - these expressions you can tell immediately from somebody’s face. The Quran highlights faces throughout this Surah, and the limbs of the faces (ears, eyes, etc.).

يَوْمَئِذٍ (yawma’idhin) [ي-و-م]

The construct يَوْمَئِذٍ means “on that day,” referring to the Day of Judgment. The word يَوْم (yawm) is in the accusative case indicating time, and إِذٍ adds the meaning of “at that time.”

خَاشِعَةٌ (khashi’ah) [خ-ش-ع]

The root خ-ش-ع is an expression for humiliation and feeling powerless, feeling overwhelmed. It actually describes something soft and relaxed, but in the context of humiliation. Khushu’ is a great quality to have when living in this life - we are supposed to try to get khushu’ in salah. But this quality is not given to good people on Judgment Day; it is flipped. It is a great quality when you are here, and it is a horrible quality on Judgment Day. The people of khushu’ will be the people in trouble with Allah. As if the people who were exhausted and humbled and overwhelmed by Allah and the fear of Him in this world will not experience that in the next, and the people who had none of that in this world will experience it in the next. Either you volunteer yourself into khushu’ in this life and it saves you from that in the next, or you don’t have any khushu’ in this life and you will feel it in the next life.

Ayah 3

عَامِلَةٌ (amilah) [ع-م-ل]

The root ع-م-ل means to work or to do. The feminine active participle عَامِلَةٌ means “working” or “laboring.” This describes faces that look like they have been working.

نَاصِبَةٌ (nasibah) [ن-ص-ب]

The root ن-ص-ب means to be exhausted, to lean over, or to peg into the ground. Someone who has exhausted themselves completely. نَصَب and تَعَب in Arabic are used for great exhaustion. This is the same word used for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم when he is commanded to exhaust himself in worship (فَإِذَا فَرَغْتَ فَانصَبْ). Here it describes that Judgment Day just begins, and you would think people were sleeping in their graves so they had a really long rest, but now they wake up on Judgment Day looking exhausted and overworked. Why? Because the time they had in their grave was not a relaxation for the disbeliever - even that time was an exhaustion for them, and they wake up exhausted from it. This is a subtle allusion in the Quran to the life that takes place inside the grave.

Ayah 4

تَصْلَىٰ (tasla) [ص-ل-ي]

The root ص-ل-ي means to be cast into fire, to be roasted or burned. The verb form تَصْلَىٰ indicates they will be cast into and experience the fire directly.

نَارًا (naran) [ن-و-ر]

The root ن-و-ر (with و as the middle radical) gives نَار (nar), meaning fire.

حَامِيَةً (hamiyah) [ح-م-ي]

The root ح-م-ي means to be scorching hot, to burn intensely. حَمِيَة (hamiyah) in Arabic is scorching, like when the sun starts burning the skin. The idea of hamiyah is extreme heat. You might think it obvious that fire scorches, so why say “scorching fire”? But this adjective suggests it could not be any hotter - it has reached maximum temperature. The fire has been prevented from going any further in its heat; it is at the extreme.

Ayah 5

تُسْقَىٰ (tusqa) [س-ق-ي]

The root س-ق-ي means to give drink, to water. The passive form تُسْقَىٰ means “they will be given to drink.”

عَيْنٍ (aynin) [ع-ي-ن]

The root ع-ي-ن gives عَيْن (ayn), which has over 70 different meanings in Arabic. It is used for the eye, and for anything beautiful - anything that captures the Arab eye they called ayn. When you hear عَيْن in this ayah, you might think there is relief because it refers to a wellspring, and the most beautiful thing for the Arab living in the desert is to see a waterfall or spring. So the word عَيْن gives relief initially.

آنِيَةٍ (aniyah) [أ-ن-ي]

The root أ-ن-ي means to reach the highest boiling point. آنِيَة (aniyah) refers to water that has reached the height of boiling. Water heats slowly, but when the bubbles become extremely violent and you say “now, now, turn it off” - that “now” moment is called أَنَى. Here it describes wellsprings of water that are scorching and bubbling. The faces that were burning are now being given “relief” by having their entire face forced into a spring that is boiling. There is another reading (تَصْطَلِي مِنْ عَيْنٍ آنِيَةٍ) which means they will voluntarily throw their faces into the boiling water because the fire is so intense.

Ayah 6

طَعَامٌ (ta’am) [ط-ع-م]

The root ط-ع-م relates to food. طَعَام (ta’am) in Arabic is not just any food (which would be أَكْل), but specifically food that nourishes, food that feeds you. They will not have anything that nourishes them.

ضَرِيعٍ (dari’) [ض-ر-ع]

The root ض-ر-ع gives ضَرِيع (dari’), which has several interpretations. Some say it is raw seaweed. A stronger interpretation found in Arabic literature is that it refers to a thorny, itchy plant from the onion family (شِبْرِق). When fresh, camels can eat it, but when it dries up, it becomes poisonous, tastes extremely bitter, and can cause extreme stomach pain and death. It is just lying around in the desert. The dried-up version of this plant, when still moist, is food for animals, but when dry it is unpleasant and even poisonous. They are force-fed something horrible both immediately and in long-term effect. Some also said this is a proper name of the fruit that comes from a tree in Hell called الزَّقُّوم (mentioned elsewhere in the Quran), so the tree here refers to the source.

Ayah 7

يُسْمِنُ (yusminu) [س-م-ن]

The root س-م-ن means to fatten, to make fat, or to fill. The negated form لَا يُسْمِنُ means it does not fatten, does not make them fat, does not fill their stomach. No matter how disgusting and poisonous the dari’ is, no matter how painful, they keep stuffing their mouths with it, and it does not help.

يُغْنِي (yughni) [غ-ن-ي]

The root غ-ن-ي means to make rich, to satisfy, to make free from need. Here لَا يُغْنِي means it does not alleviate them, does not free them from starving hunger. It does not help even a little bit.

جُوعٍ (ju’in) [ج-و-ع]

The root ج-و-ع means hunger. Their appetite does not go down even a little bit; the starving hunger remains.

Ayah 8

وُجُوهٌ (wujuhun) [و-ج-ه]

The same root و-ج-ه as before. Now the camera turns to contrast - some other faces.

نَاعِمَةٌ (na’imah) [ن-ع-م]

The root ن-ع-م gives نَاعِمَة (na’imah), which means soft, relaxed, comforted. It describes faces that are comfortable and at ease. When you look at something and it comforts you, you can see the comfort and relaxation on the face. It is also the expression on your face when you look at something beautiful. The other meaning of na’imah is when you experience luxury - both relaxation and luxury. It is the face you make when you receive a gift you really wanted.

Ayah 9

لِسَعْيِهَا (lisa’yiha) [س-ع-ي]

The root س-ع-ي means to strive, to put effort into something urgent. سَعْي (sa’y) is urgent effort. These faces that are now so happy - why? Because of the efforts these faces made, they are completely content. These were the same faces that were stressed out about obeying Allah, not crossing the line with Allah, not doing haram. They were the ones stressed in this world, they were عَامِلَة and نَاصِبَة in this world, they had khushu’ in this world. They put a lot of effort (sa’y) into just staying out of trouble in this world. Because of all those efforts today, they are pleased. The لِ (li) here is لَام السَّبَب (lam as-sabab), meaning “because of” those efforts. If you could look back and see what stress these faces had when they lived in this life, what they put up with, you would know this is so worth it.

رَاضِيَةٌ (radiyah) [ر-ض-ي]

The root ر-ض-ي means to be pleased, satisfied, content. رَاضِيَة means pleased or satisfied. The word رِضَا usually comes with بِ (bi), so you would expect رَاضِيَة بِسَعْيِهَا (pleased with their efforts). But it is لِسَعْيِهَا - because of the efforts they made, they are happy. They are not happy with their efforts; they are happy that they got to make those efforts. The لِ can mean “because of” or “for.” The implication is they are very pleased with Allah Who gave them the honor and opportunity to make efforts. The fact that we got to make efforts was a gift from Allah, and we are very happy with Allah that we got those struggles. Right now when we go through struggles we feel like complaining, but on that Day we will be thanking Allah that we went through struggles, because it is those struggles that brought us to where we are in the Akhirah.

Ayah 10

جَنَّةٍ (jannah) [ج-ن-ن]

The root ج-ن-ن gives جَنَّة (jannah), meaning garden. The root idea is of covering or concealment (related to جِنّ and جُنُون), so a garden is called jannah because the trees and greenery cover and conceal what is inside.

عَالِيَةٍ (aliyah) [ع-ل-و]

The root ع-ل-و means to be high, elevated. عَالِيَة means high, elevated. They are in a garden that is completely covered in greenery and is at a very high place. A high garden has a better view - everywhere you see is an entire heavenly landscape, and everything given to you is in your view. Elevated seating in ancient times is associated with the throne, with royalty - you are being treated like royalty.

Ayah 11

لَا تَسْمَعُ (la tasma’u) [س-م-ع]

The root س-م-ع means to hear. The negated form لَا تَسْمَعُ means “you do not hear” or “they do not hear” depending on the referent.

لَاغِيَةً (laghiyah) [ل-غ-و]

The root ل-غ-و gives لَغْو (laghw), meaning vain talk, nonsense, speech that has no benefit. لَاغِيَة is vain or useless speech. There are two interpretations: (1) The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم is told directly that in Jannah you will hear no nonsense - right now you hear terrible things when you preach, but a time will come where you will never have to hear any nonsense again. By extension, this applies to all of us. (2) Those faces (وُجُوه) will not hear any nonsense. This indicates that Jannah is not about imaginary things but about things that are real, and nothing without benefit will have a place there. At that time, all impurities will be removed, so the things we talk about will only be elevated. Everything in Jannah, even our pleasures and temptations, will have benefit. There is no greed, no jealousy, no ill feeling (وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِي صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ) - all of it will be gone.

Ayah 12

عَيْنٌ (aynun) [ع-ي-ن]

The same root ع-ي-ن as before, but here referring to a beautiful wellspring (not boiling like before).

جَارِيَةٌ (jariyah) [ج-ر-ي]

The root ج-ر-ي means to flow, to run. جَارِيَة means flowing. The wellspring we saw before was boiling and scorching; now Allah says it could not be flowing any more beautifully. You are sitting by a wellspring and it is flowing. The idea of flowing is freshness - the Arabs would be desperate to find water, and if they found a well with stale water, that would be death. So the idea of freshness is mentioned here. You can imagine splashing your face and enjoying fresh flowing water.

Ayah 13

سُرُرٌ (sururun) [س-ر-ر]

The root س-ر-ر gives سَرِير (sarir, singular) and سُرُر (surur, plural), meaning couches or beds.

مَرْفُوعَةٌ (marfu’ah) [ر-ف-ع]

The root ر-ف-ع means to raise, elevate. مَرْفُوعَة means elevated, raised. They will find couches and beds that have been elevated, again suggesting you can view the entire landscape. The Arabs did not have very elevated furniture because they were often on the move and had to pack and travel. But now you get proper, luxurious furniture. Even though the idea of bedding already implies being raised somewhat, what can also mean مَرْفُوعَة رَفْعًا is to elevate to show you how beautiful and luxurious it will be. Raised seating is associated with the throne and royalty - you are treated like royalty.

Ayah 14

أَكْوَابٌ (akwabun) [ك-و-ب]

The root ك-و-ب gives كُوب (kub, singular) and أَكْوَاب (akwab, plural), meaning cups. Specifically, these are expensive cups, goblets - golden or silver cups, really expensive ornamented cups.

مَوْضُوعَةٌ (mawdu’ah) [و-ض-ع]

The root و-ض-ع means to place, put down. مَوْضُوعَة means placed, set down. The cups are already placed, suggesting you are sitting on your couch and you want to drink something - you do not have to get up, the drinks are already placed there. Other places in the Quran fill in the picture that servants come running and pour it for you. When cups are placed, it describes a luxurious setting where you do not have to get up and serve yourself; you will be served. The reservation has already been made, the cups are already waiting for you.

Ayah 15

نَمَارِقُ (namariq) [ن-م-ر-ق]

The root ن-م-ر-ق gives نُمْرُقَة (numruqah, singular) and نَمَارِق (namariq, plural), meaning cushions or pillows.

مَصْفُوفَةٌ (masfu fah) [ص-ف-ف]

The root ص-ف-ف means to arrange in rows. مَصْفُوفَة means arranged in rows, lined up. Pillows laid out in rows - rows and rows of cushions. If you have a lot of pillows laid out, that means a lot of guests. A lot of cups, a lot of guests. One of the joys of Jannah will be company - people you actually enjoy being around. People will be facing each other, talking, asking questions, having fun conversations, enjoying drinks, with the young servants coming to fill up the drinks. This is the scene of these pillows laid out - everybody is chilling, it is a party by a waterfall, faces are lit up, a beautiful scene.

Ayah 16

زَرَابِيُّ (zarabiyyu) [ز-ر-ب]

The root ز-ر-ب is not originally Arabic. زَرَابِيّ (zarabiyy) refers to luxurious rugs, specifically imported rugs. In Arabic, they would import rugs from Azerbaijan, known for making luxurious rugs and woodcraft. Luxury items were imported - like nowadays Persian rugs or Italian cars. This is the Quran’s expression for Persian rugs. Allah is not saying in Jannah you get stuff from Azerbaijan, but He is capturing the imagination of the people living at that time and saying: imagine imported rugs you have never seen before, the closest thing you can imagine is the stuff you get from Azerbaijan right now. This is not the only place the Quran does this - at the end of Surah Ar-Rahman, Allah says فِيهِنَّ خَيْرَاتٌ حِسَانٌ and خَيْرَات عَبْقَرِيَّات, where عَبْقَرِيَّات refers to the mystical land of jinn. You have decorative items so exotic you ask “where did you get this from? This is from another world.” That is their way of saying “it is out of this world.”

مَبْثُوثَةٌ (mabthuthah) [ب-ث-ث]

The root ب-ث-ث means to spread out, scatter. مَبْثُوثَة means spread out, laid out. Rugs spread out everywhere.

Ayah 17

أَفَلَا (afala) [ف-ل-ا]

The أَ is an interrogative particle, فَ is “so” or “then,” and لَا is “not.” Together أَفَلَا means “so do they not…?” or “haven’t they…?”

يَنظُرُونَ (yanzurun) [ن-ظ-ر]

The root ن-ظ-ر means to look, see, observe. يَنظُرُونَ means “they look” or “they observe.” This is not casual looking but a good staring look, contemplation.

الْإِبِلِ (al-ibil) [أ-ب-ل]

The root أ-ب-ل gives إِبِل (ibil), meaning camels (collective noun). This captures the imagination of the Arab traveler. When do they have time to stare at a camel for a long time? When they are traveling - hours, days, weeks, months go by and all they look at is the camel in front of them, the one they are riding. They notice different things about the camel they would not otherwise notice: how gentle it is, how high the ride is, how it can survive on nothing, how it gives milk, skin, meat. This ship was created perfectly designed to navigate the desert journey - a horse would not survive. The things created inside the camel, how rough its skin is - even if the camel dies, its skin can be used for a tent to survive a sandstorm. Its meat helps them survive for weeks. Its eyes and eyelids - when sandstorms come, Arabs must cover their eyes, but the camel cannot cover its eyes and must keep moving. Allah created a car-wiper system - the eyelid blocks sand, and the camel does not have to blink in a sandstorm. This is an incredible mechanical design of this creature for your purpose.

خُلِقَتْ (khuliqat) [خ-ل-ق]

The root خ-ل-ق means to create. خُلِقَتْ is the passive perfect verb meaning “it was created.” How was the camel created? Allah knows exactly what this creature needs to survive, what it requires for this journey. By telling the Arab traveler to reflect on the camel, Allah is teaching humility: look at this marvelous creature Allah put to your service, yet you refuse to put yourself in My service.

Ayah 18

السَّمَاءِ (as-sama’) [س-م-و]

The root س-م-و gives سَمَاء (sama’), meaning sky or heaven. Anything elevated or high.

رُفِعَتْ (rufi’at) [ر-ف-ع]

The root ر-ف-ع means to raise. رُفِعَتْ is the passive perfect meaning “it was raised.” How high it has been elevated. The theme of journey continues - when will they have time to stare at the sky? When they are traveling. You have this image of someone riding on their camel, appreciating the camel, then looking up at this remarkable endless skyscape. Who put this beautiful roof over you?

Ayah 19

الْجِبَالِ (al-jibal) [ج-ب-ل]

The root ج-ب-ل gives جَبَل (jabal, singular) and جِبَال (jibal, plural), meaning mountains.

نُصِبَتْ (nusibat) [ن-ص-ب]

The root ن-ص-ب (the same root as نَاصِبَة from Ayah 3) means to peg in, to ground, to exhaust. نُصِبَتْ is the passive perfect meaning “they were pegged in” or “they were grounded.” As your eye comes down from the sky and the camel, you look far ahead and see a range of mountains. Didn’t they look at the mountains how they have been pegged into the ground? It is a repeat of the same word - faces will be exhausted (نَاصِبَة) on that Day. The arrogant ones think they will never experience that exhaustion, but Allah by implication is teaching: I can even exhaust the mountains, what are you? I have put the mountains down, I can put you down. The word نَصَب means something put down and made prominent, so it also means to raise your eyes from below.

Ayah 20

الْأَرْضِ (al-ard) [أ-ر-ض]

The root أ-ر-ض gives أَرْض (ard), meaning earth, land, ground.

سُطِحَتْ (sutihat) [س-ط-ح]

The root س-ط-ح means to spread out, flatten, make level. سُطِحَتْ is the passive perfect meaning “it was flattened” or “it was laid out flat.” Nobody appreciates flat land more than the desert traveler among the Arabs. This is the reality all around them captured: traveling with their camel, the sky above, mountains in the distance, and the flat land Allah has made level for them. This is reminiscent of Surah Al-Infitar, which talks about the sky being torn open (إِذَا السَّمَاءُ انفَطَرَتْ) and mountains sailing away (وَإِذَا الْجِبَالُ سُيِّرَتْ) and the she-camel walking off (وَإِذَا الْعِشَارُ عُطِّلَتْ). The same items repeat here as if Judgment Day will come and all these things will not be as they are now. But for now they are the way they are - you still have time, you are still on a journey where you can fix things. A time will come where the earth will not be smooth and calm like now, the sky will not be beautiful like this, the mountains will not stay in place, the camel will not be humble to you - it will be left alone. All of it will change. By implication, they are being told: you are on this journey, but you do not have a lot of time. Think about the fact that just like you go on a journey from point A to point B, your entire life is actually a journey.

Ayah 21

فَذَكِّرْ (fadhakkir) [ذ-ك-ر]

The root ذ-ك-ر means to remember, mention, remind. ذَكِّرْ is the imperative (command form) of the second form verb, meaning “so remind” or “therefore remind.” The فَ (fa) means “so” or “therefore,” connecting this command to what came before.

إِنَّمَا (innama)

This is a restriction particle composed of إِنَّ (verily) and مَا (only), meaning “only” or “verily only,” emphasizing exclusivity.

مُذَكِّرٌ (mudhakkir) [ذ-ك-ر]

From the same root ذ-ك-ر, this is the active participle of the second form, meaning “one who reminds” or “a reminder.” Your only responsibility - what are you if not a reminder, a person to give reminder? That is all you are and that is all you need to do. You do not need to change anybody. If they are not going to think for themselves, if they are not going to look at reality around them, then your reminder will have no benefit, and that is okay. But that does not change what you are supposed to do - you just keep giving a reminder. The previous Surah also said سَبِّحِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ الْأَعْلَى - it does not matter who listens or does not listen, you just remind if the reminder itself is beneficial. Here again the recipient is not specified, and Allah is saying whether they benefit or not is on them. If they become people of thought, your reminder will become a benefit to them. The subject of the reminder is not specified - should you remind them of the Akhirah, of Allah, of how they were created, of the history of previous nations? It is all open-ended. Whatever reminder you give them, if they are people of thought, all of it will converge and make sense to them. Otherwise, you cannot control them.

Ayah 22

لَسْتَ (lasta) [ل-ي-س]

لَيْسَ is a verb of negation meaning “is not” or “are not.” لَسْتَ is the second person masculine singular form: “you are not.”

عَلَيْهِم (alayhim) [ع-ل-ي]

The preposition عَلَى means “upon” or “over,” and هِم is the third person masculine plural pronoun “them.” Together: “over them.”

بِمُصَيْطِرٍ (bimusaytir) [س-ي-ط-ر]

The root س-ي-ط-ر means to have authority over, to control, to dominate. مُصَيْطِر is an active participle meaning “one who has authority” or “controller.” The بِ is a preposition that can emphasize the predicate. سَيْطَرَة means to safeguard someone, to control someone, to have complete say over somebody. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم is told: you are not in charge, you have no control over them. You cannot tell them what to do. All you can do is give a reminder. This is the attitude of a da’i - we do not control people, we do not get frustrated that they do not listen. Our job is just to give a reminder. Whether people change or not is between them and Allah. Sometimes you give a reminder to a family member and they do not listen, and you think “I even gave them the ayah properly and they did not listen.” Prophets gave reminders far better than you and people did not listen to them, so calm down. It becomes about our own pride more than the reminder itself. Changing people is up to Allah, and Allah will not change them until يُغَيِّرُوا مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ - until they change what is inside themselves. That is the message of the Surah. If their view has not changed, if the way they look at reality has not changed, then none of these reminders will change anything inside of them. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم is relieved of this responsibility.

Ayah 23

إِلَّا (illa)

This is the particle of exception meaning “except.”

تَوَلَّىٰ (tawalla) [و-ل-ي]

The root و-ل-ي in the fifth form (تَفَعَّلَ pattern) means to turn away, to turn one’s back. تَوَلَّىٰ means “he turned away.”

كَفَرَ (kafara) [ك-ف-ر]

The root ك-ف-ر means to cover, conceal, disbelieve, reject. كَفَرَ means “he disbelieved” or “he rejected.”

Ayah 24

فَيُعَذِّبُهُ (fayuadhdhibuhu) [ع-ذ-ب]

The root ع-ذ-ب means to punish, torment. يُعَذِّبُ is the imperfect verb meaning “He will punish,” and the هُ pronoun means “him.” The فَ means “then” or “so.”

اللَّهُ (Allah)

The proper name of God, the One True God.

الْعَذَابَ (al-adhab) [ع-ذ-ب]

From the same root ع-ذ-ب, this is the noun meaning “the punishment” or “the torment.”

الْأَكْبَرَ (al-akbar) [ك-ب-ر]

The root ك-ب-ر means to be great, large, big. أَكْبَر is the elative (superlative) form meaning “greater” or “greatest.” Why the greatest punishment? One interpretation: you have no authority over them now, but the ones who turn away and disbelieve - you will eventually have authority over them. Makkah will be conquered. Not only will you have authority over them, and if they still do not believe, then Allah will punish them with the ultimate punishment on top of that. Another interpretation: this is only saying Allah will deal with them exceptionally in the Akhirah. A third consideration: the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وسلم is the ultimate gift of Allah to humanity. The people who rejected him after meeting him, living with him, hearing him, spending life with him - they have committed a greater crime than anybody else who will ever commit a crime. People who come 300 years later and do not believe are still criminal but nowhere near the people who lived with him, were family with him, knew him, and still rejected him. When they turned their back on him and disbelieved despite two things - the greatest word of Allah was given, and it was given to the greatest example ever given to humanity - the greatest opportunity to accept the truth was presented to them not just in theory but in practice before their eyes. When they rejected that and became enemies against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم, there is no greater crime than that. Allah is not describing everybody in humanity here - He is actually referring specifically to those who gave the reminder, yet they remained enemies. Those are the people who deserve the greatest punishment. This makes you sensitive to how Allah talks about punishment in the Quran - every time Allah talks about punishment, He is not talking about your non-Muslim neighbor or just anybody; it is specific to a specific case.

Ayah 25

إِنَّ (inna)

This is the emphatic particle meaning “indeed” or “verily.”

إِلَيْنَا (ilayna) [أ-ل-ي]

The preposition إِلَى means “to” or “toward,” and نَا is the first person plural pronoun “us.” Together: “to Us.”

إِيَابَهُمْ (iyabahum) [أ-و-ب]

The root أ-و-ب means to return, come back. إِيَاب is the verbal noun meaning “return” or “coming back.” The هُم suffix means “their.” إِيَاب means to come back humbly, to come back with humility. They are going to be humbled and coming back to Us. There is nowhere else they can go. Let them go wherever they want, let them make fun of what you are saying, let them dismiss you, let them become your enemy - it is okay. Eventually they are coming, and there is nowhere else they can go.

Ayah 26

ثُمَّ (thumma)

This particle means “then” and indicates sequence, often with a sense of delay or importance.

عَلَيْنَا (alayna) [ع-ل-ي]

The preposition عَلَى means “upon” or “on,” and نَا is “us.” Together: “upon Us.”

حِسَابَهُمْ (hisabahum) [ح-س-ب]

The root ح-س-ب means to count, reckon, calculate, account. حِسَاب is the verbal noun meaning “reckoning” or “accounting.” The هُم suffix means “their.” Not only will they be coming back to Us with humility, it is only Us Who has taken the responsibility of auditing them, holding them to account for everything they have done. We are the only ones Who will be passing judgment over them, the only ones holding them to account.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 1

The interrogative هَلْ with أَتَاكَ creates a rhetorical question that is not seeking information but rather compelling the listener to reconsider what they already know. This is a common device in Arabic rhetoric where a question format is used to emphasize and draw attention to a matter rather than to genuinely inquire about it.

The shift from third person (الْغَاشِيَةِ) to second person (أَتَاكَ) creates intimacy and urgency - the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم is being directly addressed, yet the entire audience can hear. This is a psychological communication device where Allah expresses His concern to His Messenger صلى الله عليه وسلم as if to say: “Haven’t I done enough to teach you this? Haven’t they heard enough already? Yet it still does not get through to them.” Allah is addressing His Messenger instead of addressing the disbelievers directly, which actually expresses anger and frustration at those who refuse to listen.

The definite article in الْغَاشِيَةِ suggests this is a known event, something already established in the divine decree, lending it certainty and inevitability.

Ayah 2

The indefinite وُجُوهٌ (some faces) is significant - it is not “the faces” (all faces) but “some faces,” immediately creating a division and contrast that will be developed throughout the Surah. The tanwin (nunation) on وُجُوهٌ creates the effect of unspecified plurality - we don’t know exactly who these faces belong to yet, creating suspense.

The use of يَوْمَئِذٍ (on that day) is a common Quranic construction using the accusative of time, with the إِذْ adding specificity - not just any day, but THAT specific Day.

The feminine singular adjective خَاشِعَةٌ agreeing with وُجُوهٌ follows the rule where plural non-human or body parts are treated as feminine singular in agreement. The tanwin on خَاشِعَةٌ adds to the effect of indefiniteness and intensity - an undefined, overwhelming degree of humiliation.

Ayah 3

The two active participles عَامِلَةٌ نَاصِبَةٌ appear without a verb, creating a nominal sentence that emphasizes the state and condition rather than an action. This is called جُمْلَة اسْمِيَّة (nominal sentence) which emphasizes permanence and continuity of state.

The use of two near-synonymous words (عَامِلَةٌ نَاصِبَةٌ) is a rhetorical device of emphasis through pairing. Both words relate to exhaustion and labor, but together they intensify the image - not just working, but exhaustingly, overwhelmingly working.

Ayah 4

The passive voice تَصْلَىٰ (they will be cast) removes the agent - we know the result but the focus is on the action happening to them rather than who is doing it. This is more terrifying as it suggests inevitability without escape.

The indefinite نَارًا (a fire) with the adjective حَامِيَةً creates tamkin (تَمْكِين) - a rhetorical device where the indefiniteness of the noun allows the adjective to have maximum impact. “A fire - and what a fire! - a scorching one!”

Ayah 5

The passive تُسْقَىٰ (they will be given to drink) again removes agency and emphasizes what is happening to these faces rather than who is doing it.

The preposition مِنْ before عَيْنٍ indicates the source - they will drink FROM a spring. The indefiniteness of عَيْنٍ followed by the adjective آنِيَةٍ creates the same tamkin effect as before - “a spring - and what a spring! - a boiling one!”

The ironic contrast is powerful: عَيْن usually brings to mind relief and beauty, but آنِيَة immediately reverses that expectation. This is called مُخَالَفَة التَّوَقُّع (violating expectation) - a powerful rhetorical device.

Ayah 6

The negation لَيْسَ is the strongest form of negation in Arabic, more emphatic than لَا. It completely denies existence or possibility.

The use of لَهُمْ (for them) with the pronoun referring back to the faces creates cohesion and maintains focus on these specific individuals.

The indefiniteness of طَعَامٌ suggests “not any food whatsoever” - absolute negation of nourishment.

The exception particle إِلَّا restricts and limits - only one thing, nothing else. This creates a sense of desperation and limitation.

The word ضَرِيعٍ is indefinite and undefined, adding to its mysterious and frightening nature - something unknown and dreadful.

Ayah 7

The double negation لَا يُسْمِنُ وَلَا يُغْنِي creates powerful emphasis through repetition. The وَ (wa) coordinates two negations, reinforcing total uselessness.

The prepositional phrase مِنْ جُوعٍ (from hunger) with مِن indicating separation emphasizes that it does not free them from hunger even a little bit. The indefiniteness of جُوعٍ suggests any degree of hunger whatsoever.

The use of سَرْد (listing) - mentioning multiple negations one after another - creates a sense of comprehensive, total failure of this “food” to provide any benefit.

Ayah 8

The repetition of وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ creates parallel structure (مُوَازَنَة) with Ayah 2, but now we await a contrasting description. This parallelism followed by contrast is a powerful rhetorical device called طِبَاق (antithesis).

The indefinite نَاعِمَةٌ parallels خَاشِعَةٌ structurally but contrasts semantically - soft/comfortable vs. humbled/exhausted. This is طِبَاق إيجَابِي (positive antithesis) where opposite meanings are directly contrasted.

Ayah 9

The preposition لِ in لِسَعْيِهَا is لَام السَّبَب (lam of cause), meaning “because of” or “due to.” This is crucial - they are not pleased WITH their efforts (بِسَعْيِهَا) but pleased BECAUSE OF their efforts, or pleased that they got to make efforts.

The feminine singular رَاضِيَةٌ agrees with وُجُوهٌ following the same grammatical rule as before.

The possessive pronoun in سَعْيِهَا creates irtibat (ارْتِبَاط - connection) back to the faces, maintaining cohesion.

The nominal sentence وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَاعِمَةٌ لِسَعْيِهَا رَاضِيَةٌ has no verb, emphasizing the permanent state of these faces rather than a temporary action.

Ayah 10

The preposition فِي indicates they are IN the garden, not just visiting it - permanent residence.

The indefinite جَنَّةٍ with the adjective عَالِيَةٍ again uses tamkin for emphasis - “a garden - what a garden! - an elevated one!”

Ayah 11

The negation لَا تَسْمَعُ is less emphatic than لَيْسَ, but still creates strong negation. The ambiguity of the subject (you/they) is intentional - it could refer to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم directly or to those faces. This ambiguity (إبْهَام) allows multiple layers of meaning.

The indefinite لَاغِيَةً is accusative as the object of تَسْمَعُ. The indefiniteness suggests “any vain speech whatsoever.”

The use of فِيهَا (in it) with the pronoun referring back to جَنَّة creates cohesion and connection.

Ayah 12

The indefinite عَيْنٌ جَارِيَةٌ parallels عَيْنٍ آنِيَةٍ from earlier, but now the adjective brings relief instead of horror. This is another example of طِبَاق (antithesis) - the same noun (spring) but opposite qualities (flowing beautifully vs. boiling).

The active participle جَارِيَةٌ emphasizes the continuous, ongoing action of flowing - not stagnant, always fresh and moving.

Ayah 13

The indefinite plural سُرُرٌ followed by the passive participle مَرْفُوعَةٌ creates tamkin - “couches - and what couches! - elevated ones!”

The passive voice in مَرْفُوعَةٌ suggests they have been prepared and raised by someone (Allah and His angels) for the inhabitants.

Ayah 14

The continued use of indefinite plurals (أَكْوَابٌ, نَمَارِقُ, زَرَابِيُّ) with passive participles (مَوْضُوعَةٌ, مَصْفُوفَةٌ, مَبْثُوثَةٌ) creates a rhythmic pattern. This is called سَجْع (saj’ - rhymed prose), where the verse endings create a pleasing auditory pattern.

The passive participles emphasize that everything has been prepared - the inhabitants do not have to do anything, everything is ready and waiting for them.

Ayah 15

The passive participle مَصْفُوفَةٌ (arranged in rows) suggests order, organization, and abundance - not just a few pillows scattered around, but many arranged beautifully.

Ayah 16

The passive participle مَبْثُوثَةٌ (spread out) suggests luxurious abundance - rugs everywhere, not just in one place.

The plurals with matching passive participles create a list (سَرْد) that builds up the image of luxury and comfort piece by piece.

Ayah 17

The interrogative particle أَفَلَا (أَ + فَ + لَا) combines several elements: أَ for question, فَ for “so/then” (indicating connection to what preceded), and لَا for negation. Together this creates “so do they not…?” or “haven’t they…?” This is not a genuine question but تَوْبِيخ (rebuke) - a rhetorical question meant to scold and make them think.

The change to third person يَنظُرُونَ (they look) after addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم in second person shifts the focus to the audience - those who refuse to think and reflect.

The preposition إِلَى indicates direction of sight - looking AT or TOWARD something with focus and attention.

The use of الْإِبِلِ (camels) as the first item is significant because it is closest to them - they ride it, live with it, depend on it. This is called تَدَرُّج (gradation) - starting with what is nearest and most familiar.

كَيْفَ (kayfa)

The interrogative كَيْفَ (how) asks about manner and condition - not just THAT it was created, but HOW, in what marvelous way.

The passive خُلِقَتْ removes the creator from mention, but any Arab would know the implied agent is Allah. This is called حَذْف الْفَاعِل (omission of the agent) for emphasis on the creation itself and its marvelous nature.

Ayah 18

The وَ (wa) at the beginning connects this to the previous ayah, continuing the series of rhetorical questions. The parallel structure أَفَلَا يَنظُرُونَ… وَإِلَى السَّمَاءِ… وَإِلَى الْجِبَالِ… وَإِلَى الْأَرْضِ creates a rhythmic pattern (تَوَازُن - balance/parallelism).

The progression is significant: from the camel (closest, on the ground with them) to the sky (furthest, above), creating a vertical movement of the gaze that encompasses all of creation around them.

The passive رُفِعَتْ (it was raised) again omits the agent, focusing on the miraculous nature of the sky’s elevation.

Ayah 19

The passive نُصِبَتْ (they were pegged down) continues the pattern of passive verbs, emphasizing the created order rather than the Creator (though He is implied).

The repetition of the root ن-ص-ب (which appeared in نَاصِبَة in Ayah 3) creates a subtle verbal connection - faces will be exhausted (نَاصِبَة), yet the mountains were firmly established (نُصِبَتْ). This is a form of جِنَاس (wordplay/paronomasia) where the same root appears in different contexts with related meanings.

Ayah 20

The passive سُطِحَتْ (it was flattened) concludes the series of four rhetorical questions, all using passive verbs, creating a unified rhetorical structure.

The progression from camel (animal, close, moving) to sky (far, high, fixed) to mountains (solid, grounded, permanent) to earth (beneath them, spread out, supporting) encompasses the entire visible world around the Arab traveler. This is called إِحَاطَة (encompassing) - covering all aspects of the surrounding reality.

Ayah 21

The فَ (fa) at the beginning of فَذَكِّرْ creates a logical conclusion (نَتِيجَة) from what preceded - “since they can see all this evidence around them, therefore remind them.”

The imperative ذَكِّرْ is a command, direct and unambiguous, addressed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم.
